prompt: In the 2015 Metropolitan Museum of Art exhibition titled after the Chinese zodiac animal of 2015, how many of the "twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac" have a hand visible?
actual:
**11** of the twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac have a hand visible.

The 2015 Metropolitan Museum of Art exhibition was titled "Celebration of the Year of the Ram" (2015 being the Year of the Ram/Goat in the Chinese zodiac). Among the works displayed was a set of twelve jade (nephrite) zodiac figurines from the Qing dynasty (18th–19th century), catalogued as "Twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac" (accession number 02.18.730a–l, object ID 42102).

Each figurine depicts an anthropomorphic animal-headed figure in scholarly robes. Visual analysis of the set confirms that 11 of the 12 figures have visible hands emerging from their robes, holding various attributes (scrolls, fans, staffs, etc.). Only the **Snake** figurine has its hands completely hidden inside its robe sleeves, with no hands visible.

Animals with visible hands: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, Pig (11).
Animal without visible hands: Snake (1).
finish_summary:
**11** of the twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac have a hand visible.

The 2015 Metropolitan Museum of Art exhibition was titled "Celebration of the Year of the Ram" (2015 being the Year of the Ram/Goat in the Chinese zodiac). input.prompt: This is a group photo of 12 jade Chinese zodiac animal figurines from the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Can you describe each animal figure? Which ones have visible hands or arms? List each of the 12 animals and whether a hand is visible.
This is a jade Chinese zodiac figurine from the Met Museum. Which animal is this? Is there a visible hand? Describe what you see.

Be very specific about whether the Snake (6th position, middle row, 2nd from left) has visible hands or not. Also check each animal carefully.

input.thought:

1. The 2015 Met Museum exhibition was "Celebration of the Year of the Ram"
2. It featured a set of 12 jade Chinese zodiac figurines (object ID 42102, "Twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac", Qing dynasty, 18th-19th century, nephrite jade)
3. Two independent visual analyses of the group photo confirmed:
   - 11 of the 12 zodiac animals have visible hands (emerging from their robes, holding objects)
   - Only the Snake has its hands hidden inside its robes with no hands visible

Therefore, 11 out of 12 have a hand visible.

Each figurine depicts an anthropomorphic animal-headed figure in scholarly robes. Visual analysis of the set confirms that 11 of the 12 figures have visible hands emerging from their robes, holding various attributes (scrolls, fans, staffs, etc.). Only the **Snake** figurine has its hands completely hidden inside its robe sleeves, with no hands visible.
